summary: Gruber reports on confusion among developers after Apple sent an ambiguous message about their iPhone SDK beta program applications. The email left developers unsure whether it was a receipt confirmation or a rejection. Gruber notes that even top indie Mac developers received the same message, suggesting it's either a blanket form letter or Apple is being extremely selective. He contextualizes the issue by explaining that while anyone can develop for the iPhone simulator, the beta program is needed to test on actual hardware, which matters significantly for apps using OpenGL, Core Location, or the accelerometer.
key_points: Apple sent an ambiguous form letter to iPhone SDK beta applicants that could be interpreted as either acknowledgment or rejection
Even prominent indie Mac developers ('top men') received the identical message, suggesting it's not targeted rejection
The simulator has significant limitations — no OpenGL, no Core Location, no accelerometer — making device testing essential
The long-term developer program will likely be inclusive, but the beta period's exclusivity remains an open question
money_quotes: No one seems to know whether this is a 'we got your application' form letter, or a 'you've been rejected, at least for now' form letter.
Either way, the message is poorly phrased.
I have firsthand confirmation that some of the biggest names in indie Mac development — Raiders of the Lost Ark-style 'top men' — received this exact same message.

So, either it's just a form letter sent to all developers who've applied to let them know Apple is still reviewing their applications, or else Apple is being extraordinarily selective about the first wave of developers in the beta program.
The question is how exclusive the developer program will be during the beta period.
key_insight: Apple's poor communication about the iPhone SDK beta program left even top-tier developers confused about their status, highlighting the company's tendency toward opacity in developer relations during the iPhone platform's critical early days.
